result:
`strategy.md` is written. Here's what I concluded and where I pushed back.

## The core read

The pile looks contradictory but converges hard once you separate signal from noise. Lumen and Competitor A are **mirror images**: you win on front-end routing, you lose on the back-end ERP handoff. So the whole strategy is — protect the half you win, fix the half you lose.

**The retention leak is entirely on the back end.** Every product-driven churn happens at the ERP handoff, never at OCR or routing. 3 of 5 real churns name re-keying; it's the biggest support theme (98 tickets); the market is consolidating around "post to the ERP without re-keying"; Competitor A already shipped it. NRR 108→97 *is* this leak.

**Routing is your moat and it's under-invested.** Strongest retention signal, held across cohorts and controlling for size, drives expansion (Orsino) and activation (first-14-day rule → 6-month retention).

## The two bets
1. **Close the loop into the ERP** (two-way native sync, NetSuite → Intacct) — the retention fix.
2. **Turn routing into a moat** (conditional/per-vendor rules, approval limits, delegation, routing-led onboarding) — the expansion engine, cheaper and faster.

## Where I said no
- **Enterprise pipeline (Atlas/Meridian)** — loudest, least likely to close, no finance champion, ARR is AE fiction. Declined as roadmap drivers. (Caveat: SOC 2 / SSO as a background trust track, not one of your two eng bets.)
- **AI Spend Copilot** — loud but wrong buyer/wrong job; declined the race.
- **Mobile, reporting dashboards, OCR-as-a-bet, self-serve growth** — de-prioritized or killed.

## The internal belief I called wrong
That the enterprise logos are the growth story — they're a time sink. And **stop putting "+34% QoQ" in the board deck**: 38% is one logo auto-importing, 22% is self-serve accounts where nobody approves. Real mid-market growth is ~6%. The vanity metric is hiding the exact problem you're trying to solve.

## Sequencing
Both start Q1; routing wins land first (cheaper, de-risks the half). NetSuite sync gated on two design partners — Tindall (at-risk, 90-day renewal) and Dovetail — surviving a real month-end close before GA, because Greenfield churned when the handoff broke *during* close. If forced into a true either/or: **ERP sync wins** — you can't expand a bucket draining out the back.

Two things I flagged as assumptions worth a 5-minute check: NetSuite-before-Intacct depends on your actual install base, and whether SOC 2 can progress without eating a core eng bet.

One question that would sharpen this further if you want to go deeper: do you know the NetSuite vs. Intacct split across your 140 paying accounts? That's the one fact that would lock the Bet 1 sequence.
